After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.

1	---
2	title: Caching and Revalidating
3	description: Learn how to cache and revalidate data in your application.
4	related:
5	  title: API Reference
6	  description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference.
7	  links:
8	    - app/api-reference/functions/fetch
9	    - app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag
10	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
11	    - app/api-reference/functions/updateTag
12	    - app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath
13	    - app/api-reference/functions/unstable_cache
14	---
15	
16	Caching is a technique for storing the result of data fetching and other computations so that future requests for the same data can be served faster, without doing the work again. While revalidation allows you to update cache entries without having to rebuild your entire application.
17	
18	Next.js provides a few APIs to handle caching and revalidation. This guide will walk you through when and how to use them.
19	
20	- [`fetch`](#fetch)
21	- [`cacheTag`](#cachetag)
22	- [`revalidateTag`](#revalidatetag)
23	- [`updateTag`](#updatetag)
24	- [`revalidatePath`](#revalidatepath)
25	- [`unstable_cache`](#unstable_cache) (Legacy)
26	
27	## `fetch`
28	
29	By default, [`fetch`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) requests are not cached. You can cache individual requests by setting the `cache` option to `'force-cache'`.
30	
31	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
32	export default async function Page() {
33	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
34	}
35	```
36	
37	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
38	export default async function Page() {
39	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { cache: 'force-cache' })
40	}
41	```
42	
43	> **Good to know**: Although `fetch` requests are not cached by default, Next.js will [pre-render](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) routes that have `fetch` requests and cache the HTML. If you want to guarantee a route is [dynamic](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), use the [`connection` API](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection).
44	
45	To revalidate the data returned by a `fetch` request, you can use the `next.revalidate` option.
46	
47	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher
48	export default async function Page() {
49	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
50	}
51	```
52	
53	```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher
54	export default async function Page() {
55	  const data = await fetch('https://...', { next: { revalidate: 3600 } })
56	}
57	```
58	
59	This will revalidate the data after a specified amount of seconds.
60	
61	You can also tag `fetch` requests to enable on-demand cache invalidation:
62	
63	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
64	export async function getUserById(id: string) {
65	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
66	    next: {
67	      tags: ['user'],
68	    },
69	  })
70	}
71	```
72	
73	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
74	export async function getUserById(id) {
75	  const data = await fetch(`https://...`, {
76	    next: {
77	      tags: ['user'],
78	    },
79	  })
80	}
81	```
82	
83	See the [`fetch` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/fetch) to learn more.
84	
85	## `cacheTag`
86	
87	[`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) allows you to tag cached data in [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) so it can be revalidated on-demand. Previously, cache tagging was limited to `fetch` requests, and caching other work required the experimental `unstable_cache` API.
88	
89	With Cache Components, you can use the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive to cache any computation, and `cacheTag` to tag it. This works with database queries, file system operations, and other server-side work.
90	
91	```tsx filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
92	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
93	
94	export async function getProducts() {
95	  'use cache'
96	  cacheTag('products')
97	
98	  const products = await db.query('SELECT * FROM products')
99	  return products
100	}
101	```
102	
103	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
104	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
105	
106	export async function getProducts() {
107	  'use cache'
108	  cacheTag('products')
109	
110	  const products = await db.query('SELECT * FROM products')
111	  return products
112	}
113	```
114	
115	Once tagged, you can use [`revalidateTag`](#revalidatetag) or [`updateTag`](#updatetag) to invalidate the cache entry for products.
116	
117	> **Good to know**: `cacheTag` is used with [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) and the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive. It expands the caching and revalidation story beyond `fetch`.
118	
119	See the [`cacheTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) to learn more.
120	
121	## `revalidateTag`
122	
123	`revalidateTag` is used to revalidate cache entries based on a tag and following an event. The function now supports two behaviors:
124	
125	- **With `profile="max"`**: Uses stale-while-revalidate semantics, serving stale content while fetching fresh content in the background
126	- **Without the second argument**: Legacy behavior that immediately expires the cache (deprecated)
127	
128	After tagging your cached data, using [`fetch`](#fetch) with `next.tags`, or the [`cacheTag`](#cachetag) function, you may call `revalidateTag` in a [Route Handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) or Server Action:
129	
130	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1,5} switcher
131	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
132	
133	export async function updateUser(id: string) {
134	  // Mutate data
135	  revalidateTag('user', 'max') // Recommended: Uses stale-while-revalidate
136	}
137	```
138	
139	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1,5} switcher
140	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
141	
142	export async function updateUser(id) {
143	  // Mutate data
144	  revalidateTag('user', 'max') // Recommended: Uses stale-while-revalidate
145	}
146	```
147	
148	You can reuse the same tag in multiple functions to revalidate them all at once.
149	
150	See the [`revalidateTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) to learn more.
151	
152	## `updateTag`
153	
154	`updateTag` is specifically designed for Server Actions to immediately expire cached data for read-your-own-writes scenarios. Unlike `revalidateTag`, it can only be used within Server Actions and immediately expires the cache entry.
155	
156	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1,6} switcher
157	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
158	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
159	
160	export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
161	  // Create post in database
162	  const post = await db.post.create({
163	    data: {
164	      title: formData.get('title'),
165	      content: formData.get('content'),
166	    },
167	  })
168	
169	  // Immediately expire cache so the new post is visible
170	  updateTag('posts')
171	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
172	
173	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
174	}
175	```
176	
177	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1,6} switcher
178	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
179	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
180	
181	export async function createPost(formData) {
182	  // Create post in database
183	  const post = await db.post.create({
184	    data: {
185	      title: formData.get('title'),
186	      content: formData.get('content'),
187	    },
188	  })
189	
190	  // Immediately expire cache so the new post is visible
191	  updateTag('posts')
192	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
193	
194	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
195	}
196	```
197	
198	The key differences between `revalidateTag` and `updateTag`:
199	
200	- **`updateTag`**: Only in Server Actions, immediately expires cache, for read-your-own-writes
201	- **`revalidateTag`**: In Server Actions and Route Handlers, supports stale-while-revalidate with `profile="max"`
202	
203	See the [`updateTag` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) to learn more.
204	
205	## `revalidatePath`
206	
207	`revalidatePath` is used to revalidate a route and following an event. To use it, call it in a [Route Handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) or Server Action:
208	
209	```tsx filename="app/lib/actions.ts" highlight={1} switcher
210	import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache'
211	
212	export async function updateUser(id: string) {
213	  // Mutate data
214	  revalidatePath('/profile')
215	```
216	
217	```jsx filename="app/lib/actions.js" highlight={1} switcher
218	import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache'
219	
220	export async function updateUser(id) {
221	  // Mutate data
222	  revalidatePath('/profile')
223	```
224	
225	See the [`revalidatePath` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) to learn more.
226	
227	## `unstable_cache`
228	
229	> **Good to know**: `unstable_cache` is an experimental API. We recommend opting into [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) and replacing `unstable_cache` with the [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) directive. See the [Cache Components documentation](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) for more details.
230	
231	`unstable_cache` allows you to cache the result of database queries and other async functions. To use it, wrap `unstable_cache` around the function. For example:
232	
233	```ts filename="app/lib/data.ts" switcher
234	import { db } from '@/lib/db'
235	export async function getUserById(id: string) {
236	  return db
237	    .select()
238	    .from(users)
239	    .where(eq(users.id, id))
240	    .then((res) => res[0])
241	}
242	```
243	
244	```jsx filename="app/lib/data.js" switcher
245	import { db } from '@/lib/db'
246	
247	export async function getUserById(id) {
248	  return db
249	    .select()
250	    .from(users)
251	    .where(eq(users.id, id))
252	    .then((res) => res[0])
253	}
254	```
255	
256	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={2,11,13} switcher
257	import { unstable_cache } from 'next/cache'
258	import { getUserById } from '@/app/lib/data'
259	
260	export default async function Page({
261	  params,
262	}: {
263	  params: Promise<{ userId: string }>
264	}) {
265	  const { userId } = await params
266	
267	  const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
268	    async () => {
269	      return getUserById(userId)
270	    },
271	    [userId] // add the user ID to the cache key
272	  )
273	}
274	```
275	
276	```jsx filename="app/page.js" highlight={2,7,9} switcher
277	import { unstable_cache } from 'next/cache'
278	import { getUserById } from '@/app/lib/data'
279	
280	export default async function Page({ params }) {
281	  const { userId } = await params
282	
283	  const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
284	    async () => {
285	      return getUserById(userId)
286	    },
287	    [userId] // add the user ID to the cache key
288	  )
289	}
290	```
291	
292	The function accepts a third optional object to define how the cache should be revalidated. It accepts:
293	
294	- `tags`: an array of tags used by Next.js to revalidate the cache.
295	- `revalidate`: the number of seconds after cache should be revalidated.
296	
297	```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" highlight={6-9} switcher
298	const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
299	  async () => {
300	    return getUserById(userId)
301	  },
302	  [userId],
303	  {
304	    tags: ['user'],
305	    revalidate: 3600,
306	  }
307	)
308	```
309	
310	```jsx filename="app/page.js" highlight={6-9} switcher
311	const getCachedUser = unstable_cache(
312	  async () => {
313	    return getUserById(userId)
314	  },
315	  [userId],
316	  {
317	    tags: ['user'],
318	    revalidate: 3600,
319	  }
320	)
321	```
322	
323	See the [`unstable_cache` API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_cache) to learn more.

278 286 287 ) 288 } 289 ``` 290 291 ## Examples 292 293 ### Showing a pending state 294 295 While executing a Server Function, you can show a loading indicator with React's [`useActionState`](https://react.dev/reference/react/useActionState) hook. This hook returns a `pending` boolean: 296 297 ```tsx filename="app/ui/button.tsx" switcher 298 'use client' 299 300 import { useActionState, startTransition } from 'react' 301 import { createPost } from '@/app/actions' 302 import { LoadingSpinner } from '@/app/ui/loading-spinner' 303 304 export function Button() { 305 const [state, action, pending] = useActionState(createPost, false) 306 307 return ( 308 311 ) 312 } 313 ``` 314 315 ```jsx filename="app/ui/button.js" switcher 316 'use client' 317 318 import { useActionState, startTransition } from 'react' 319 import { createPost } from '@/app/actions' 320 import { LoadingSpinner } from '@/app/ui/loading-spinner' 321 322 export function Button() { 323 const [state, action, pending] = useActionState(createPost, false) 324 325 return ( 326 329 ) 330 } 331 ``` 332 333 ### Refreshing 334 335 After a mutation, you may want to refresh the current page to show the latest data. You can do this by calling [`refresh`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/refresh) from `next/cache` in a Server Action: 336 337 ```ts filename="app/lib/actions.ts" switcher 338 'use server' 339 340 import { refresh } from 'next/cache' 341 342 export async function updatePost(formData: FormData) { 343 // Update data 344 // ... 345 346 refresh() 347 } 348 ``` 349 350 ```js filename="app/lib/actions.js" switcher 351 'use server' 352 353 import { refresh } from 'next/cache' 354 355 export async function updatePost(formData) { 356 // Update data 357 // ... 358 359 refresh() 360 } 361 ``` 362 363 This refreshes the client router, ensuring the UI reflects the latest state. The `refresh()` function does not revalidate tagged data. To revalidate tagged data, use [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) or [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) instead. 364 365 ### Revalidating 366 367 After performing an update, you can revalidate the Next.js cache and show the updated data by calling [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) or [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) within the Server Function: 368 369 ```ts filename="app/lib/actions.ts" switcher 370 import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache' 371 372 export async function createPost(formData: FormData) { 373 'use server' 374 // Update data 375 // ... 376 377 revalidatePath('/posts') 378 } 379 ``` 380 381 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 382 import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache' 383 384 export async function createPost(formData) { 385 'use server' 386 // Update data 387 // ... 388 revalidatePath('/posts') 389 } 390 ``` 391 392 ### Redirecting 393 394 You may want to redirect the user to a different page after performing an update. You can do this by calling [`redirect`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/redirect) within the Server Function. 395 396 ```ts filename="app/lib/actions.ts" switcher 397 'use server' 398 399 import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache' 400 import { redirect } from 'next/navigation' 401 402 export async function createPost(formData: FormData) { 403 // Update data 404 // ... 405 406 revalidatePath('/posts') 407 redirect('/posts') 408 } 409 ``` 410 411 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 412 'use server' 413 414 import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache' 415 import { redirect } from 'next/navigation' 416 417 export async function createPost(formData) { 418 // Update data 419 // ... 420 421 revalidatePath('/posts') 422 redirect('/posts') 423 } 424 ``` 425 426 Calling `redirect` [throws](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/redirect#behavior) a framework handled control-flow exception. Any code after it won't execute. If you need fresh data, call [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) or [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) beforehand. 427 428 ### Cookies 429 430 You can `get`, `set`, and `delete` cookies inside a Server Action using the [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) API. 431 432 When you [set or delete](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies#understanding-cookie-behavior-in-server-functions) a cookie in a Server Action, Next.js re-renders the current page and its layouts on the server so the **UI reflects the new cookie value**. 433 434 > **Good to know**: The server update applies to the current React tree, re-rendering, mounting, or unmounting components, as needed. Client state is preserved for re-rendered components, and effects re-run if their dependencies changed. 435 436 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 437 'use server' 438 439 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 440 441 export async function exampleAction() { 442 const cookieStore = await cookies() 443 444 // Get cookie 445 cookieStore.get('name')?.value 446 447 // Set cookie 448 cookieStore.set('name', 'Delba') 449 450 // Delete cookie 451 cookieStore.delete('name') 452 } 453 ``` 454 455 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 456 'use server' 457 458 import { cookies } from 'next/headers' 459 460 export async function exampleAction() { 461 // Get cookie 462 const cookieStore = await cookies() 463 464 // Get cookie 465 cookieStore.get('name')?.value 466 467 // Set cookie 468 cookieStore.set('name', 'Delba') 469 470 // Delete cookie 471 cookieStore.delete('name') 472 } 473 ``` 474 475 ### useEffect 476 477 You can use the React [`useEffect`](https://react.dev/reference/react/useEffect) hook to invoke a Server Action when the component mounts or a dependency changes. This is useful for mutations that depend on global events or need to be triggered automatically. For example, `onKeyDown` for app shortcuts, an intersection observer hook for infinite scrolling, or when the component mounts to update a view count: 478 479 ```tsx filename="app/view-count.tsx" switcher 480 'use client' 481 482 import { incrementViews } from './actions' 483 import { useState, useEffect, useTransition } from 'react' 484 485 export default function ViewCount({ initialViews }: { initialViews: number }) { 486 const [views, setViews] = useState(initialViews) 487 const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition() 488 489 useEffect(() => { 490 startTransition(async () => { 491 const updatedViews = await incrementViews() 492 setViews(updatedViews) 493 }) 494 }, []) 495 496 // You can use `isPending` to give users feedback 497 return

Total Views: {views}

498 } 499 ``` 500 501 ```jsx filename="app/view-count.js" switcher 502 'use client' 503 504 import { incrementViews } from './actions' 505 import { useState, useEffect, useTransition } from 'react' 506 507 export default function ViewCount({ initialViews }) { 508 const [views, setViews] = useState(initialViews) 509 const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition() 510 511 useEffect(() => { 512 startTransition(async () => { 513 const updatedViews = await incrementViews() 514 setViews(updatedViews) 515 }) 516 }, []) 517 518 // You can use `isPending` to give users feedback 519 return

2	title: updateTag
3	description: API Reference for the updateTag function.
4	---
5	
6	`updateTag` allows you to update [cached data](/docs/app/guides/caching) on-demand for a specific cache tag from within [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data).
7	
8	This function is designed for **read-your-own-writes** scenarios, where a user makes a change (like creating a post), and the UI immediately shows the change, rather than stale data.
9	
10	## Usage
11	
12	`updateTag` can **only** be called from within [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data). It cannot be used in Route Handlers, Client Components, or any other context.
13	
14	If you need to invalidate cache tags in Route Handlers or other contexts, use [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) instead.
15	
16	> **Good to know**: `updateTag` immediately expires the cached data for the specified tag. The next request will wait to fetch fresh data rather than serving stale content from the cache, ensuring users see their changes immediately.
17	
18	## Parameters
19	
20	```tsx
21	updateTag(tag: string): void;
22	```
23	
24	- `tag`: A string representing the cache tag associated with the data you want to update. Must not exceed 256 characters. This value is case-sensitive.
25	
26	Tags must first be assigned to cached data. You can do this in two ways:
27	
28	- Using the [`next.tags`](/docs/app/guides/caching#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) option with `fetch` for caching external API requests:
29	
30	```tsx
31	fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['posts'] } })
32	```
33	
34	- Using [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) inside cached functions or components with the `'use cache'` directive:
35	
36	```tsx
37	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
38	
39	async function getData() {
40	  'use cache'
41	  cacheTag('posts')
42	  // ...
43	}
44	```
45	
46	## Returns
47	
48	`updateTag` does not return a value.
49	
50	## Differences from revalidateTag
51	
52	While both `updateTag` and `revalidateTag` invalidate cached data, they serve different purposes:
53	
54	- **`updateTag`**:
55	  - Can only be used in Server Actions
56	  - Next request waits for fresh data (no stale content served)
57	  - Designed for read-your-own-writes scenarios
58	
59	- **`revalidateTag`**:
60	  - Can be used in Server Actions and Route Handlers
61	  - With `profile="max"` (recommended): Serves cached data while fetching fresh data in the background (stale-while-revalidate)
62	  - With custom profile: Can be configured to any cache life profile for advanced usage
63	  - Without profile: legacy behavior which is equivalent to `updateTag`
64	
65	## Examples
66	
67	### Server Action with Read-Your-Own-Writes
68	
69	```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher
70	'use server'
71	
72	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
73	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
74	
75	export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
76	  const title = formData.get('title')
77	  const content = formData.get('content')
78	
79	  // Create the post in your database
80	  const post = await db.post.create({
81	    data: { title, content },
82	  })
83	
84	  // Invalidate cache tags so the new post is immediately visible
85	  // 'posts' tag: affects any page that displays a list of posts
86	  updateTag('posts')
87	  // 'post-{id}' tag: affects the individual post detail page
88	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
89	
90	  // Redirect to the new post - user will see fresh data, not cached
91	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
92	}
93	```
94	
95	```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher
96	'use server'
97	
98	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
99	import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
100	
101	export async function createPost(formData) {
102	  const title = formData.get('title')
103	  const content = formData.get('content')
104	
105	  // Create the post in your database
106	  const post = await db.post.create({
107	    data: { title, content },
108	  })
109	
110	  // Invalidate cache tags so the new post is immediately visible
111	  // 'posts' tag: affects any page that displays a list of posts
112	  updateTag('posts')
113	  // 'post-{id}' tag: affects the individual post detail page
114	  updateTag(`post-${post.id}`)
115	
116	  // Redirect to the new post - user will see fresh data, not cached
117	  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`)
118	}
119	```
120	
121	### Error when used outside Server Actions
122	
123	```ts filename="app/api/posts/route.ts" switcher
124	import { updateTag } from 'next/cache'
125	
126	export async function POST() {
127	  // This will throw an error
128	  updateTag('posts')
129	  // Error: updateTag can only be called from within a Server Action
130	
131	  // Use revalidateTag instead in Route Handlers
132	  revalidateTag('posts', 'max')
133	}
134	```
135	
136	## When to use updateTag
137	
138	Use `updateTag` when:
139	
140	- You're in a Server Action
141	- You need immediate cache invalidation for read-your-own-writes
142	- You want to ensure the next request sees updated data
143	
144	Use `revalidateTag` instead when:
145	
146	- You're in a Route Handler or other non-action context
147	- You want stale-while-revalidate semantics
148	- You're building a webhook or API endpoint for cache invalidation
149	
150	## Related
151	
152	- [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) - For invalidating tags in Route Handlers
153	- [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) - For invalidating specific paths

13	
14	The `cacheTag` function allows you to tag cached data for on-demand invalidation. By associating tags with cache entries, you can selectively purge or revalidate specific cache entries without affecting other cached data.
15	
16	## Usage
17	
18	To use `cacheTag`, enable the [`cacheComponents` flag](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) in your `next.config.js` file:
19	
20	```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher
21	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
22	
23	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
24	  cacheComponents: true,
25	}
26	
27	export default nextConfig
28	```
29	
30	```js filename="next.config.js" switcher
31	const nextConfig = {
32	  cacheComponents: true,
33	}
34	
35	export default nextConfig
36	```
37	
38	The `cacheTag` function takes one or more string values.
39	
40	```tsx filename="app/data.ts" switcher
41	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
42	
43	export async function getData() {
44	  'use cache'
45	  cacheTag('my-data')
46	  const data = await fetch('/api/data')
47	  return data
48	}
49	```
50	
51	```jsx filename="app/data.js" switcher
52	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
53	
54	export async function getData() {
55	  'use cache'
56	  cacheTag('my-data')
57	  const data = await fetch('/api/data')
58	  return data
59	}
60	```
61	
62	You can then purge the cache on-demand using [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) API in another function, for example, a [route handler](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) or [Server Action](/docs/app/getting-started/updating-data):
63	
64	```tsx filename="app/action.ts" switcher
65	'use server'
66	
67	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
68	
69	export default async function submit() {
70	  await addPost()
71	  revalidateTag('my-data')
72	}
73	```
74	
75	```jsx filename="app/action.js" switcher
76	'use server'
77	
78	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
79	
80	export default async function submit() {
81	  await addPost()
82	  revalidateTag('my-data')
83	}
84	```
85	
86	## Good to know
87	
88	- **Idempotent Tags**: Applying the same tag multiple times has no additional effect.
89	- **Multiple Tags**: You can assign multiple tags to a single cache entry by passing multiple string values to `cacheTag`.
90	
91	```tsx
92	cacheTag('tag-one', 'tag-two')
93	```
94	
95	- **Limits**: The max length for a custom tag is 256 characters and the max tag items is 128.
96	
97	## Examples
98	
99	### Tagging components or functions
100	
101	Tag your cached data by calling `cacheTag` within a cached function or component:
102	
103	```tsx filename="app/components/bookings.tsx" switcher
104	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
105	
106	interface BookingsProps {
107	  type: string
108	}
109	
110	export async function Bookings({ type = 'haircut' }: BookingsProps) {
111	  'use cache'
112	  cacheTag('bookings-data')
113	
114	  async function getBookingsData() {
115	    const data = await fetch(`/api/bookings?type=${encodeURIComponent(type)}`)
116	    return data
117	  }
118	
119	  return //...
120	}
121	```
122	
123	```jsx filename="app/components/bookings.js" switcher
124	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
125	
126	export async function Bookings({ type = 'haircut' }) {
127	  'use cache'
128	  cacheTag('bookings-data')
129	
130	  async function getBookingsData() {
131	    const data = await fetch(`/api/bookings?type=${encodeURIComponent(type)}`)
132	    return data
133	  }
134	
135	  return //...
136	}
137	```
138	
139	### Creating tags from external data
140	
141	You can use the data returned from an async function to tag the cache entry.
142	
143	```tsx filename="app/components/bookings.tsx" switcher
144	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
145	
146	interface BookingsProps {
147	  type: string
148	}
149	
150	export async function Bookings({ type = 'haircut' }: BookingsProps) {
151	  async function getBookingsData() {
152	    'use cache'
153	    const data = await fetch(`/api/bookings?type=${encodeURIComponent(type)}`)
154	    cacheTag('bookings-data', data.id)
155	    return data
156	  }
157	  return //...
158	}
159	```
160	
161	```jsx filename="app/components/bookings.js" switcher
162	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
163	
164	export async function Bookings({ type = 'haircut' }) {
165	  async function getBookingsData() {
166	    'use cache'
167	    const data = await fetch(`/api/bookings?type=${encodeURIComponent(type)}`)
168	    cacheTag('bookings-data', data.id)
169	    return data
170	  }
171	  return //...
172	}
173	```
174	
175	### Invalidating tagged cache
176	
177	Using [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag), you can invalidate the cache for a specific tag when needed:
178	
179	```tsx filename="app/actions.ts" switcher
180	'use server'
181	
182	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
183	
184	export async function updateBookings() {
185	  await updateBookingData()
186	  revalidateTag('bookings-data')
187	}
188	```
189	
190	```jsx filename="app/actions.js" switcher
191	'use server'
192	
193	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
194	
195	export async function updateBookings() {
196	  await updateBookingData()
197	  revalidateTag('bookings-data')
198	}
199	```

2	title: revalidateTag
3	description: API Reference for the revalidateTag function.
4	---
5	
6	`revalidateTag` allows you to invalidate [cached data](/docs/app/guides/caching) on-demand for a specific cache tag.
7	
8	This function is ideal for content where a slight delay in updates is acceptable, such as blog posts, product catalogs, or documentation. Users receive stale content while fresh data loads in the background.
9	
10	## Usage
11	
12	`revalidateTag` can be called in Server Functions and Route Handlers.
13	
14	`revalidateTag` cannot be called in Client Components or Proxy, as it only works in server environments.
15	
16	### Revalidation Behavior
17	
18	The revalidation behavior depends on whether you provide the second argument:
19	
20	- **With `profile="max"` (recommended)**: The tag entry is marked as stale, and the next time a resource with that tag is visited, it will use stale-while-revalidate semantics. This means the stale content is served while fresh content is fetched in the background.
21	- **With a custom cache life profile**: For advanced usage, you can specify any cache life profile that your application has defined, allowing for custom revalidation behaviors tailored to your specific caching requirements.
22	- **Without the second argument (deprecated)**: The tag entry is expired immediately, and the next request to that resource will be a blocking revalidate/cache miss. This behavior is now deprecated, and you should either use `profile="max"` or migrate to [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag).
23	
24	> **Good to know**: When using `profile="max"`, `revalidateTag` marks tagged data as stale, but fresh data is only fetched when pages using that tag are next visited. This means calling `revalidateTag` will not immediately trigger many revalidations at once. The invalidation only happens when any page using that tag is next visited.
25	
26	## Parameters
27	
28	```ts
29	revalidateTag(tag: string, profile: string | { expire?: number }): void;
30	```
31	
32	- `tag`: A string representing the cache tag associated with the data you want to revalidate. Must not exceed 256 characters. This value is case-sensitive.
33	- `profile`: A string that specifies the revalidation behavior. The recommended value is `"max"` which provides stale-while-revalidate semantics, or any of the other default or custom profiles defined in [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheLife). Alternatively, you can pass an object with an `expire` property for custom expiration behavior.
34	
35	Tags must first be assigned to cached data. You can do this in two ways:
36	
37	- Using the [`next.tags`](/docs/app/guides/caching#fetch-optionsnexttags-and-revalidatetag) option with `fetch` for caching external API requests:
38	
39	```tsx
40	fetch(url, { next: { tags: ['posts'] } })
41	```
42	
43	- Using [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) inside cached functions or components with the `'use cache'` directive:
44	
45	```tsx
46	import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache'
47	
48	async function getData() {
49	  'use cache'
50	  cacheTag('posts')
51	  // ...
52	}
53	```
54	
55	> **Good to know**: The single-argument form `revalidateTag(tag)` is deprecated. It currently works if TypeScript errors are suppressed, but this behavior may be removed in a future version. Update to the two-argument signature.
56	
57	## Returns
58	
59	`revalidateTag` does not return a value.
60	
61	## Relationship with `revalidatePath`
62	
63	`revalidateTag` invalidates data with specific tags across all pages that use those tags, while [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath) invalidates specific page or layout paths.
64	
65	> **Good to know**: These functions serve different purposes and may need to be used together for comprehensive data consistency. For detailed examples and considerations, see [relationship with revalidateTag and updateTag](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath#relationship-with-revalidatetag-and-updatetag) for more information.
66	
67	## Examples
68	
69	The following examples demonstrate how to use `revalidateTag` in different contexts. In both cases, we're using `profile="max"` to mark data as stale and use stale-while-revalidate semantics, which is the recommended approach for most use cases.
70	
71	### Server Action
72	
73	```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher
74	'use server'
75	
76	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
77	
78	export default async function submit() {
79	  await addPost()
80	  revalidateTag('posts', 'max')
81	}
82	```
83	
84	```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher
85	'use server'
86	
87	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
88	
89	export default async function submit() {
90	  await addPost()
91	  revalidateTag('posts', 'max')
92	}
93	```
94	
95	### Route Handler
96	
97	```ts filename="app/api/revalidate/route.ts" switcher
98	import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
99	import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache'
100	
101	export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
102	  const tag = request.nextUrl.searchParams.get('tag')
103	
104	  if (tag) {
105	    revalidateTag(tag, 'max')
106	    return Response.json({ revalidated: true, now: Date.now() })
107	  }
108

1	---
2	title: cacheComponents
3	description: Learn how to enable the cacheComponents flag in Next.js.
4	---
5	
6	The `cacheComponents` flag is a feature in Next.js that causes data fetching operations in the App Router to be excluded from pre-renders unless they are explicitly cached. This can be useful for optimizing the performance of dynamic data fetching in Server Components.
7	
8	It is useful if your application requires fresh data fetching during runtime rather than serving from a pre-rendered cache.
9	
10	It is expected to be used in conjunction with [`use cache`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) so that your data fetching happens at runtime by default unless you define specific parts of your application to be cached with `use cache` at the page, function, or component level.
11	
12	## Usage
13	
14	To enable the `cacheComponents` flag, set it to `true` in your `next.config.ts` file:
15	
16	```ts filename="next.config.ts"
17	import type { NextConfig } from 'next'
18	
19	const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
20	  cacheComponents: true,
21	}
22	
23	export default nextConfig
24	```
25	
26	When `cacheComponents` is enabled, you can use the following cache functions and configurations:
27	
28	- The [`use cache` directive](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache)
29	- The [`cacheLife` function](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheLife) with `use cache`
30	- The [`cacheTag` function](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag)
31	
32	## Notes
33	
34	- While `cacheComponents` can optimize performance by ensuring fresh data fetching during runtime, it may also introduce additional latency compared to serving pre-rendered content.
35	
36	## Version History
37	
38	| Version | Change                                                                                                                            |
39	| ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
40	| 16.0.0  | `cacheComponents` introduced. This flag controls the `ppr`, `useCache`, and `dynamicIO` flags as a single, unified configuration. |

It can be used at the top of a file to indicate that all exports in the file should be cached, or inline at the top of function or component to cache the return value. 18 19 > **Good to know:** 20 > 21 > - To use cookies or headers, read them outside cached scopes and pass values as arguments. This is the preferred pattern. 22 > - If the in-memory cache isn't sufficient for runtime data, [`'use cache: remote'`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache-remote) allows platforms to provide a dedicated cache handler, though it requires a network roundtrip to check the cache and typically incurs platform fees. 23 > - For compliance requirements or when you can't refactor to pass runtime data as arguments to a `use cache` scope, see [`'use cache: private'`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache-private). 24 25 ## Usage 26 27 `use cache` is a Cache Components feature. To enable it, add the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) option to your `next.config.ts` file: 28 29 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 30 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 31 32 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 33 cacheComponents: true, 34 } 35 36 export default nextConfig 37 ``` 38 39 ```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 40 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 41 const nextConfig = { 42 cacheComponents: true, 43 } 44 45 module.exports = nextConfig 46 ``` 47 48 Then, add `use cache` at the file, component, or function level: 49 50 ```tsx 51 // File level 52 'use cache' 53 54 export default async function Page() { 55 // ... 56 } 57 58 // Component level 59 export async function MyComponent() { 60 'use cache' 61 return <> 62 } 63 64 // Function level 65 export async function getData() { 66 'use cache' 67 const data = await fetch('/api/data') 68 return data 69 } 70 ``` 71 72 > **Good to know**: When used at file level, all function exports must be async functions. 73 74 ## How `use cache` works 75 76 ### Cache keys 77 78 A cache entry's key is generated using a serialized version of its inputs, which includes: 79 80 1. **Build ID** - Unique per build, changing this invalidates all cache entries 81 2. **Function ID** - A secure hash of the function's location and signature in the codebase 82 3. **Serializable arguments** - Props (for components) or function arguments 83 4. **HMR refresh hash** (development only) - Invalidates cache on hot module replacement 84 85 When a cached function references variables from outer scopes, those variables are automatically captured and bound as arguments, making them part of the cache key. 86 87 ```tsx filename="lib/data.ts" 88 async function Component({ userId }: { userId: string }) { 89 const getData = async (filter: string) => { 90 'use cache' 91 // Cache key includes both userId (from closure) and filter (argument) 92 return fetch(`/api/users/${userId}/data?filter=${filter}`) 93 } 94 95 return getData('active') 96 } 97 ``` 98 99 In the snippet above, `userId` is captured from the outer scope and `filter` is passed as an argument, so both become part of the `getData` function's cache key. This means different user and filter combinations will have separate cache entries. 100 101 ## Serialization 102 103 Arguments to cached functions and their return values must be serializable. 104 105 For a complete reference, see: 106 107 - [Serializable arguments](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-server#serializable-parameters-and-return-values) - Uses **React Server Components** serialization 108 - [Serializable return types](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client#serializable-types) - Uses **React Client Components** serialization 109 110 > **Good to know:** Arguments and return values use different serialization systems. Server Component serialization (for arguments) is more restrictive than Client Component serialization (for return values). This means you can return JSX elements but cannot accept them as arguments unless using pass-through patterns. 111 112 ### Supported types 113 114 **Arguments:** 115 116 - Primitives: `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `null`, `undefined` 117 - Plain objects: `{ key: value }` 118 - Arrays: `[1, 2, 3]` 119 - Dates, Maps, Sets, TypedArrays, ArrayBuffers 120 - React elements (as pass-through only) 121 122 **Return values:** 123 124 - Same as arguments, plus JSX elements 125 126 ### Unsupported types 127 128 - Class instances 129 - Functions (except as pass-through) 130 - Symbols, WeakMaps, WeakSets 131 - URL instances 132 133 ```tsx filename="app/components/user-card.tsx" 134 // Valid - primitives and plain objects 135 async function UserCard({ 136 id, 137 config, 138 }: { 139 id: string 140 config: { theme: string } 141 }) { 142 'use cache' 143 return
{id}
144 } 145 146 // Invalid - class instance 147 async function UserProfile({ user }: { user: UserClass }) { 148 'use cache' 149 // Error: Cannot serialize class instance 150 return
{user.name}
151 } 152 ``` 153 154 ### Pass-through (non-serializable arguments) 155 156 You can accept non-serializable values **as long as you don't introspect them**. This enables composition patterns with `children` and Server Actions: 157 158 ```tsx filename="app/components/cached-wrapper.tsx" 159 async function CachedWrapper({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) { 160 'use cache' 161 // Don't read or modify children - just pass it through 162 return ( 163
164
Cached Header
165 {children} 166
167 ) 168 } 169 170 // Usage: children can be dynamic 171 export default function Page() { 172 return ( 173 174 {/* Not cached, passed through */} 175 176 ) 177 } 178 ``` 179 180 You can also pass Server Actions through cached components: 181 182 ```tsx filename="app/components/cached-form.tsx" 183 async function CachedForm({ action }: { action: () => Promise }) { 184 'use cache' 185 // Don't call action here - just pass it through 186 return
{/* ... */}
187 } 188 ``` 189 190 ## Constraints 191 192 Cached functions execute in an isolated environment. The following constraints ensure cache behavior remains predictable and secure. 193 194 ### Runtime APIs 195 196 Cached functions and components **cannot** directly access runtime APIs like `cookies()`, `headers()`, or `searchParams`. Instead, read these values outside the cached scope and pass them as arguments. 197 198 ### Runtime caching considerations 199 200 While `use cache` is designed primarily to include dynamic data in the static shell, it can also cache data at runtime using in-memory LRU (Least Recently Used) storage. 201 202 Runtime cache behavior depends on your hosting environment: 203 204 | Environment | Runtime Caching Behavior | 205 | --------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 206 | **Serverless** | Cache entries typically don't persist across requests (each request can be a different instance). Build-time caching works normally. | 207 | **Self-hosted** | Cache entries persist across requests. Control cache size with [`cacheMaxMemorySize`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/incrementalCacheHandlerPath). | 208 209 If the default in-memory cache isn't enough, consider **[`use cache: remote`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache-remote)** which allows platforms to provide a dedicated cache handler (like Redis or KV database). This helps reduce hits against data sources not scaled to your total traffic, though it comes with costs (storage, network latency, platform fees). 210 211 Very rarely, for compliance requirements or when you can't refactor your code to pass runtime data as arguments to a `use cache` scope, you might need [`use cache: private`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache-private). 212 213 ### React.cache isolation 214 215 [`React.cache`](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) operates in an isolated scope inside `use cache` boundaries. Values stored via `React.cache` outside a `use cache` function are not visible inside it. 216 217 This means you cannot use `React.cache` to pass data into a `use cache` scope: 218 219 ```tsx 220 import { cache } from 'react' 221 222 const store = cache(() => ({ current: null as string | null })) 223 224 function Parent() { 225 const shared = store() 226 shared.current = 'value from parent' 227 return 228 } 229 230 async function Child() { 231 'use cache' 232 const shared = store() 233 // shared.current is null, not 'value from parent' 234 // use cache has its own isolated React.cache scope 235 return
{shared.current}
236 } 237 ``` 238 239 This isolation ensures cached functions have predictable, self-contained behavior. To pass data into a `use cache` scope, use function arguments instead. 240 241 ## `use cache` at runtime 242 243 On the **server**, cache entries are stored in-memory and respect the `revalidate` and `expire` times from your `cacheLife` configuration. You can customize the cache storage by configuring [`cacheHandlers`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheHandlers) in your `next.config.js` file. 244 245 On the **client**, content from the server cache is stored in the browser's memory for the duration defined by the `stale` time. The client router enforces a **minimum 30-second stale time**, regardless of configuration. 246 247 The `x-nextjs-stale-time` response header communicates cache lifetime from server to client, ensuring coordinated behavior. 248 249 ## Revalidation 250 251 By default, `use cache` uses the `default` profile with these settings: 252 253 - **stale**: 5 minutes (client-side) 254 - **revalidate**: 15 minutes (server-side) 255 - **expire**: Never expires by time 256 257 ```tsx filename="lib/data.ts" 258 async function getData() { 259 'use cache' 260 // Implicitly uses default profile 261 return fetch('/api/data') 262 } 263 ``` 264 265 ### Customizing cache lifetime 266 267 Use the [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife) function to customize cache duration: 268 269 ```tsx filename="lib/data.ts" 270 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 271 272 async function getData() { 273 'use cache' 274 cacheLife('hours') // Use built-in 'hours' profile 275 return fetch('/api/data') 276 } 277 ``` 278 279 ### On-demand revalidation 280 281 Use [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag), [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag), or [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) for on-demand cache invalidation: 282 283 ```tsx filename="lib/data.ts" 284 import { cacheTag } from 'next/cache' 285 286 async function getProducts() { 287 'use cache' 288 cacheTag('products') 289 return fetch('/api/products') 290 } 291 ``` 292 293 ```tsx filename="app/actions.ts" 294 'use server' 295 296 import { updateTag } from 'next/cache' 297 298 export async function updateProduct() { 299 await db.products.update(...) 300 updateTag('products') // Invalidates all 'products' caches 301 } 302 ``` 303 304 Both `cacheLife` and `cacheTag` integrate across client and server caching layers, meaning you configure your caching semantics in one place and they apply everywhere. 305 306 ## Examples 307 308 ### Caching an entire route with `use cache` 309 310 To pre-render an entire route, add `use cache` to the top of **both** the `layout` and `page` files.
